original_abstract: Hydrogen produced via zero-emission pathways, commonly known as "green hydrogen," represents a critical pillar for achieving the global decarbonization milestones established by the Paris Agreement. Among competing technologies, proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers (PEMWEs) are uniquely positioned to spearhead this transition due to their high efficiency and rapid dynamic response. However, large-scale market adoption remains limited, with global PEMWE capacity currently stalled at less than one gigawatt. To facilitate meaningful climate action, this capacity must scale to hundreds of gigawatts by 2030. This paper provides a comprehensive roadmap for the cell-level innovations required to enable such gigawatt-scale deployment, integrating strategic perspectives from three leading industrial hydrogen companies. We provide detailed multidisciplinary analyses covering economies of scale, renewable energy pricing, and evolving government policies. Furthermore, we outline scientific priorities for component-specific performance improvements and propose standardized accelerated stress tests (ASTs) to ensure the long-term durability demanded by industrial-scale operations.
humanized_abstract: Green hydrogen, produced from non-carbonaceous sources using carbon-neutral processes, will be a key element in the realization of the ambitious reductions in emissions necessary to honor the Paris Agreement. Amongst all technologies, proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers (PEMWEs) are currently leading the charge due to their high efficiency and fast transient response. The PEMWE production capacity is currently still below one gigawatt globally. In order to enable the production of the required amount of hydrogen in line with the needed climate change mitigation measures, the global capacity has to be enhanced to hundreds of gigawatts by 2030. This paper is intended to act as a seminal reference for necessary technological advancements needed to reach the required performance at the cell level, including commentary from executives of three leading industrial hydrogen production companies. The necessary considerations for each component, as well as the multidisciplinary analysis on economies of scale, renewable energy costs, and policy development are included. In addition, the necessary fundamental scientific advancements for the improvement of component performance are outlined and the recommended corresponding accelerated stress tests (ASTs) are discussed to ensure the longevity of the components at the required production scale.
